posts.title: Show HN: Proliferate- build and own workflows for any coding agent
posts.text:
Hi HN, I'm Pablo, the founder of Proliferate!

Proliferate is an open-source and self-hostable AI IDE and workflow platform for coding agents. You can use Proliferate to work with agents interactively or use them as building blocks for repeatable workflows, running locally, in the cloud, or on infrastructure you control. We support Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, Cursor, and Grok through their native harnesses, so all their native functionality and configurability carries over (goal loops, plans, your skills).

2 minute demo: [DEMO LINK]

The demo shows how I fix bugs in Proliferate using Proliferate. Whenever somebody files an issue, four things happen automatically, in sequence: 1) Codex gathers and writes up context on the issue from our logs, DB state, and issue tracker, 2) Claude Code uses that context to implement a fix, 3) a PR is automatically created, 4) 5 distinct GLM 5.2 and Claude Code sessions review the PR for compliance with repo best practices. At the end of those steps, I get pinged in Slack with what's usually a reasonable fix that I can easily review.

So far, work with coding agents has mostly meant a 'copilot' model: one person working with one agent at a time on one well-scoped task. But agents can now accomplish economically meaningful work end to end, and I think the immediate future looks like teams building 'factory lines' for the recurring work in their organization- agents taking in tasks or goals and iterating until there's something a human can review. We've already seen users do this for code review, technical support, QA, auto alert investigation, and more.

As for why we specifically exist:

I think that this layer of the agent stack, operating above the agents themselves, should be open source, self-hostable, and neutral- specifically because of a) how core this will be to how companies operate and b) these factory lines needing to work across all agents and models (including self-hosted ones).

On a): this layer holds your credentials, provisions your environments, reads your source code, and runs commands against your internal systems. If agents keep improving, it's also where most work at your company will actually happen. Your triggers, review standards, and business processes will all live there. That should be something you can self-host and own.

On b): you should maintain model neutrality in a world where there is no clear model lineage winner. You can look at recent events like Fable gating questions related to model development, or recent open source releases, and see that there will for a while longer be a proliferation of useful harnesses you'll want to embed in your workflows.

The companies furthest along on agentic adoption- Ramp, Stripe, and [xyz]- already staffed internal teams to build exactly this layer for themselves. Most companies can't spare a platform team for that, and I want everyone to have access to what those teams built internally without getting tied to a given model lineage.

If this sounds like problems you're hitting, try us out! You can self-host us with a single install script or try our hosted version.

I'd love to hear if you're already doing something like this, wht you'd need before taking the leap to self-hosting your factorylines. We're still early, so there may be bugs, but we ship a new version every day and I'll be reading comments all day.

GitHub: https://github.com/proliferate-ai/proliferate
Try it: https://proliferate.com/

comments.content:
The question of who controls the infrastructure that coordinates agent work is not primarily technical but institutional. When we talk about "factory lines" of agents handling economically meaningful work, we are describing a fundamental shift in how labor gets allocated and supervised within organizations. The tooling that orchestrates that coordination becomes a point of control over business process, not just a convenience layer.

Open source and self-hosting matter here not because of abstract principles but because of concrete interests. If the platform that schedules your agents, holds your credentials, and enforces your review policies is operated by a third party with economic incentives orthogonal to yours, you have ceded governance over critical operations. Model neutrality is similarly not just a technical hedge but a structural requirement in an ecosystem where no single foundation model vendor has achieved durable dominance. Locking workflow logic to a single harness is a category error when the underlying models are still in flux and when different tasks may call for different tool suites.

What remains unresolved is whether organizations will actually treat this layer as infrastructure worth owning, or whether convenience and velocity will push them toward managed services that externalize those concerns. The analogy to internal platform teams at Ramp or Stripe is instructive but incomplete. Those teams exist because those companies recognized that operational autonomy over critical paths is worth the overhead. Most companies have not made that determination and may not until they experience a control or compliance failure that forces the issue.

The real test is not whether this approach is technically sound, but whether it aligns incentives correctly when work itself becomes delegable to distributed agents. If it does, self-hosting becomes inevitable. If it does not, openness alone will not be enough.

comments.content:
So this is basically "we fine-tuned a mid-size video model on a small, heavily curated dataset and gave the prompts fancy names". Chain-of-frame reasoning is just latent state propagation with more verbose logging - any autoregressive or diffusion model that conditions on previous frames is already doing this. 17k videos is tiny at this scale, so what you are mostly seeing is overfitting to a narrow distribution of synthetic tasks and then getting rewarded on very similar synthetic benchmarks that know the answer format in advance.

The "visual reasoning tokens" vs "textual reasoning tokens" bit reads like standard control tokens and prompt engineering, wrapped in new terminology to sound like a novel architecture. If you can encode "object A moves left, then collides with B" in either a side-channel embedding or extra text, of course you'll get a few point bump on contrived tests. This is fine as an engineering exercise, but it is miles away from genuine temporal reasoning.

comments.content:
“Diverse genres and decades” is doing a lot of work here for 3.5 hours of audio. That sounds tiny compared to what modern AMT models are trained or evaluated on, especially given how repetitive pop is structurally. Do the authors quantify coverage in any meaningful way, e.g., distribution over instrumentation, harmonic vocab, production styles? Listing that a song is from “the 1930s” tells me nothing about whether it is a big band recording with dense horn voicings, or a sparse vocal-piano track. Without that, “diverse” is just hand-waving.  

Also, the alignment procedure is described as if it were straightforward, but that is exactly where these datasets usually fall apart. How do they validate that the beat tracking plus warping yields note-accurate alignment, not just visually plausible piano rolls? For instance, do they measure timing error between annotated onsets and audio transients on a held-out subset, or is it just “manual spot checking”? If the latter, that 38% Onset F1 may be partly dataset noise, not “room for improvement.”

comments.content:
This situation offers a revealing case study in how trust erosion works in platform-mediated commerce, particularly when intermediaries operate at scale while positioned between consumers and merchants. The shopping plugin model has always presented structural incentives for this kind of conduct. Plugins sit invisibly in the browser stack, making detection difficult and enforcement costly, while their economic model depends on maximizing attributed transactions regardless of whether genuine influence occurred.

What strikes me about the Phia case is how it reflects broader patterns in digital advertising fraud. The fundamental problem is not new since the earliest affiliate networks. But the technology evolves while enforcement mechanisms remain static. Browser extensions represent a more sophisticated vector than toolbar software from the 2000s, yet the legal frameworks and network policies have barely adapted. We see this same dynamic in ad tech generally, where sophisticated parties exploit information asymmetries and technical complexity to extract rents from less sophisticated market participants.

The historical precedents Edelman mentions are instructive because they suggest enforcement requires egregious, sustained abuse before criminal liability attaches. Civil enforcement through contract law remains weak when platforms prioritize growth over compliance monitoring. This creates a predictable cycle, grow rapidly through aggressive tactics, face eventual scrutiny, then either reform minimally or collapse while successors repeat the pattern. The structural incentives remain unchanged.

The larger question is whether affiliate marketing as currently constituted can remain viable when trust costs continue escalating. Merchants face the impossible task of distinguishing genuine customer influence from mere interception, while honest affiliates compete against those willing to bend rules. Without stronger platform accountability or reformed attribution models, we should expect more Phias, not fewer.

comments.content:
I spent about six months trying to build compliance tools for affiliate networks back in 2019 and this stuff was already a nightmare then. The incentives are just broken from the ground up. You have networks that take a cut either way, so they don't care until someone makes noise. Merchants barely understand the tech and just see sales numbers. Meanwhile affiliates race to the bottom because clean players can't compete with forced clicks and cookie stuffing.

What really got me was how many obviously shady plugins had millions of installs and glowing reviews. Users had no idea their clicks were being hijacked. I eventually gave up on that project because policing it felt impossible without cooperation from the browser vendors themselves, and they were too busy with other priorities. The Hogan and Dunning cases were supposed to scare people straight but clearly the lesson was just don't get big enough to attract FBI attention. Networks won't self police until the legal pressure outweighs the revenue they're collecting from these bad actors.

comments.content:
Forced clicks in affiliate plugins are not an aberration; they flow from last click attribution, growth dashboards, and soft enforcement. If revenue depends on being the final touch, a toolbar either honors consent and loses to rivals, or fires a quiet redirect and calls it optimization. Example the cookie, then tout user savings while stealing the referrer’s commission. Networks publish policies, then tolerate volume. Compliance is a press release, not a control system.

Users, meanwhile, outsource judgment to convenience layers that promise free money. Install, accept friendly prompts, and never inspect what happens between keystroke and receipt. That asymmetry is the point. The startup playbook is routine too if Phia is punished, the tactic persists until incentives change. The fixes are structural rather than an invisible redirect.

It's truly inspiring to see such entrepreneurial spirit channeled into circumventing the most basic tenets of fair play, rather than, say, building something genuinely useful or innovative. It speaks volumes about the priorities we've somehow cultivated in this grand digital age.

The "move fast and break things" mantra has evidently mutated into "move fast and break trust," all in the pursuit of optimizing fleeting metrics. When the pinnacle of technological ingenuity becomes figuring out new ways to trick users into generating ad revenue for negligible value, it’s not just a flaw in a plugin; it's a symptom of a deeper rot. We seem to be trading genuine creativity and the meticulous craft of building lasting value for the instant gratification of illicit clicks, further eroding the foundations of a culture that once prized competence and integrity. What a brilliant future we're architecting.

posts.title: Groundtruth - Deterministic verification for AI coding agents

comments.content:
Interesting questions about project rules – that point does seem critical for determinism. While I agree the core git diff check feels robust, I'm skeptical about how Groundtruth handles the natural language claim parsing deterministically. How does it avoid interpretation drift when mapping "I fixed the bug" to specific code changes without some form of model inference? 

Also, the "no workflow" claim puzzles me. Even a local hook requires *some* integration point. Does it truly impose zero changes, or does it just push configuration complexity elsewhere? How lightweight is that integration in practice?

comments.content:
This is just another symptom of the cargo cult around AI tooling, where we keep slapping bandaids on the fundamental unreliability of LLMs instead of questioning whether they should be writing code in the first place. The fact that we need a "deterministic truth-checker" to catch an AI's lies about its own work speaks volumes about the absurdity of the premise. We've normalized treating AI agents as if they're junior engineers who occasionally fib about their progress, rather than recognizing them as stochastic parrots with no actual understanding of their output.

The deeper irony is how this mirrors the degradation of trust in human engineering teams. Just as we've added layers of CI/CD, linters, and code reviews to compensate for the breakdown of individual accountability in large organizations, we're now building the same scaffolding for AI agents. But at least humans can theoretically be held responsible for their false "done" claims. What's the recourse when an AI lies? More tooling, apparently. It's a recursive loop of mistrust that says more about our cultural acceptance of broken processes than about any technical innovation.

What's most telling is the framing of this as a productivity win rather than an indictment. The pitch isn't "this will make your AI write better code," but rather "this will catch your AI lying faster." We've internalized the idea that unreliable outputs are inevitable, so now we compete on who can fail most gracefully. It's the same mindset that gave us microservices sprawl and dependency hell—a relentless focus on mitigating symptoms while ignoring root causes. But hey, at least it's deterministic.

comments.content:
What I find most interesting here is not the implementation, but the fact that we are starting to institutionalize "honesty" checks for non-human agents. Treating "done" as a claim and the diff as evidence is conceptually clean, but it quietly reframes software work as a sequence of verifiable assertions, which could just as easily be applied to humans. Once you have a deterministic honesty layer for agents, it is a very small organizational step to measure individual developers by the ratio of "claims" to "groundtruth" over time, even though human work is often exploratory, interrupted, and context dependent.

On the privacy front, the local-only, no-network design is genuinely positive for code confidentiality, but it does not fully answer the question of how these verdicts will be logged, aggregated, and interpreted. A persistent log of "false claims" and "rule breaks" attached to specific repositories, branches, or users can become an implicit performance dossier. As these tools spread, there is a real risk that nuanced engineering judgment is compressed into binary traffic light indicators that travel easily in management reports but strip away the surrounding context.

Fairness and agency also come into play in how "rules" are defined and enforced. If security or style policies are encoded into a deterministic gate, who gets to revise those policies, and how transparent is the process when they conflict with practical constraints? For human collaborators working alongside agents, there is a danger that disagreement with the tool's verdict is perceived as resistance to "objective truth," even though the system only checks a narrow surface (diffs, tests run, file presence) rather than intent, tradeoffs, or long term maintainability. The governance of that boundary will matter more than the cleverness of the plugin itself.

comments.content:
Nice work on this. The core insight that deterministic verification beats LLM-based code review is spot on, and the execution looks clean. Too many tools in this space try to solve the wrong problem by adding more AI when what we actually need is better truth detection.

The implementation approach is particularly solid. Running as a local git hook eliminates the network dependency issues that plague most agent tooling, and the fact that it parses actual diffs rather than asking another model to "understand" the code changes is the right architectural choice. One suggestion exposing the rule configuration more prominently in your docs since that seems like it would be the primary customization point for different codebases. Also, you might want to think about how this integrates with existing CI/CD workflows beyond just the Claude plugin. The git hook approach should make it fairly straightforward to bolt onto GitHub Actions or similar, but explicit examples would help adoption.

The "claims vs evidence" framing is exactly right and something more people should be thinking about as AI coding tools mature.

posts.title: Measuring Intelligence Beyond Human Scale

comments.content:
Back in the early days of chess engines, we ran into something similar. Once Deep Blue beat Kasparov, how exactly were we supposed to evaluate whether one superhuman chess program was actually better than another? You couldn't just watch them play and intuit which one understood the position more deeply. The solution then was pretty straightforward Elo system worked because the game had clear win conditions and you could just aggregate thousands of matches.

What strikes me about this paper is that they're trying to generalize that competitive approach to domains where verification isn't as clean as checkmate. The psychometric rating idea feels like a natural evolution, but I'm curious how they handle the fact that modern ML systems can potentially collude or game the challenge generation process in ways that 1997-era chess engines couldn't. The incentive structure gets really weird when the evaluators and evaluated are drawing from similar training distributions.

I do appreciate that they're thinking past saturation though. We spent years adding more chess puzzles and Go problems to benchmarks only to watch them get solved in a few training runs. The treadmill problem is real. If this framework can actually keep pace with capability growth without requiring constant human oversight, that would be genuinely useful. I'm skeptical about the open-ended domains, but the verifiable case seems pretty solid on first read.

Would be interested to see how this holds up when the models get good enough to find verification shortcuts we haven't thought of yet.

comments.content: The null-prompt alignment step is actually standard practice in diffusion guidance research and has been since classifier-free guidance became the norm—it's not reverse-engineering so much as exploiting the documented geometry of these models where conditioned and unconditioned trajectories naturally form a navigable manifold. In production motion synthesis pipelines (games, VFX, simulations), people have known for years that the first 10-20% of denoising steps disproportionately determine semantic coherence while later steps refine kinematic plausibility, so selectively intervening early isn't cargo cult but informed engineering based on ablation studies that consistently show this phase separation. The real insider nuance here is that motion generation hit a retraining wall not because of scale economics but because available motion-capture datasets are fundamentally too small and domain-specific (mostly lab environments, limited actors) to support the kind of world-model emergence we see in vision or language, making these retrieval-based workarounds genuinely practical compared to waiting for datasets that don't exist yet.

comments.content:
I spent six months trying to stabilize motion capture outputs for a small animation studio and ran into this exact pattern. We kept adding preprocessing steps, curated noise banks, prompt engineering tricks, and post-filtering pipelines until the actual generation became the smallest part of the workflow. What started as "just feed it text" turned into a multi-stage ritual that required its own documentation and maintenance overhead.

The really frustrating part was explaining to stakeholders why supposedly state-of-the-art models needed all this scaffolding to produce usable results. Your point about computational taxidermy resonates—we were essentially building an entire ecosystem of workarounds rather than acknowledging the models simply weren't ready for production use. Eventually we went back to traditional procedural animation with ML assist rather than the other way around.

comments.content:
This is yet another example of how the "winning ticket" concept—originally a quirky observation about neural network initialization—has metastasized into a full-blown cargo cult in machine learning research. Every week brings a new paper claiming to have discovered some latent golden sample in the noise space that unlocks hidden performance. It's the same pattern we saw with hyperparameter tuning, then architecture search, and now noise sculpting. The field's obsession with these micro-optimizations speaks to a deeper cultural issue: our collective inability to admit that current generative models are fundamentally unstable and prone to semantic drift precisely because they lack robust world models.

What's especially telling here is the need for null-prompt alignment, which essentially amounts to reverse-engineering the model's biases just to get consistent output. It's a clever hack, but also a damning indictment of how brittle these systems are. We're building increasingly ornate scaffolding around models that still can't reliably understand simple temporal sequences—something humans manage effortlessly. The fact that this approach "improves fidelity without retraining" isn't a feature; it's a symptom of how expensive and ineffective model retraining has become in this scale-driven paradigm.

The paper's framing as a "training-free solution" is particularly emblematic of modern ML's perverse incentives. Rather than addressing the core limitations of diffusion models, we get another procedural band-aid that will inevitably join the growing heap of bespoke preprocessing steps required to make generative AI behave. It's computational taxidermy—stuffing the noise space with artificial structure to prop up the illusion of control. One wonders how many more "winning tickets" we'll need before admitting the lottery itself might be flawed.

comments.content:
This sounds like overfitting with extra steps. You're literally pre-selecting noise based on which ones happen to produce motions that look right under null prompts, then claiming this improves semantic alignment. That's just cherry-picking from the prior distribution.

The real problem is that your diffusion model doesn't understand compositionality or temporal structure well enough in the first place. Searching through noise space for "winning tickets" doesn't fix the underlying failure mode where the model can't maintain semantic consistency across long sequences. It just masks it by finding lucky initializations.

Also curious how this scales. Building a retrieval index over noise samples and running KL optimization per prompt doesn't sound particularly training-free in terms of compute. You're trading gradient descent for exhaustive search.

comments.content:
I worked on a similar problem last year with diffusion models for gesture generation. We noticed that certain noise seeds consistently produced more coherent sequences, almost like they had an inherent bias toward natural motion.  

Tried brute-forcing it by sampling hundreds of noise vectors and scoring them against a kinematic smoothness metric. It worked, but was painfully slow. WINRO’s approach of mapping noise to null-prompt features seems like a smarter way to pre-select candidates.  

Curious how it handles edge cases though—we hit scenarios where even “good” noise would occasionally derail after 5-6 seconds.

comments.content:
Tried something similar last year with a diffusion model for gesture generation. Spent weeks tweaking noise seeds only to realize the "winning tickets" were just overfitting to our tiny test set—real users still got janky, incoherent motions. Feels like another case of academic metrics masking practical failure modes.  

The whole "latent structure in noise" thing reminds me of when we thought GANs had meaningful latent walks. Turns out most of it was just us seeing patterns in randomness. I’ll believe this works when it survives a month in prod without constant babysitting.

posts.title: Agentic Data Environments

comments.content:
The persistent fascination with "autonomous agents" and their "substantial gains" seems to overlook the historical pattern of complex systems. The notion of "bounding the consequences of failure" when agents operate over a data environment spanning "files, APIs, applications, and system state" feels less like a solution and more like a recasting of an intractable problem. We consistently underestimate the emergent properties of interconnected systems, and the idea that we can simply build in safety guarantees for something so expansive feels like a familiar, perhaps even comforting, delusion.

If these environments are truly giving agents access to such a broad swathe of our digital lives, then the implications for privacy are profound and concerning. It's not merely about data being stored anymore; it's about an active, autonomous entity sifting through, interpreting, and potentially acting upon every facet of our digital existence. What does consent even mean in such a context, when the 'active substrate' is constantly making decisions about our information? This shift feels like a fundamental erosion of individual control over personal data, moving us further towards a reality where our digital selves are perpetually exposed to automated scrutiny.

Furthermore, the redefinition of data systems into "active substrates for safe, reliable execution" raises immediate questions about agency and fairness. Whose definition of "safe" and "reliable" will be embedded into these systems, and what happens when those definitions inevitably clash with individual needs or broader societal equity? We know algorithmic systems tend to amplify biases present in their training data, and giving them active decision-making power across such a wide domain only guarantees that those biases will be enacted at scale, often without clear avenues for human oversight or recourse. The promise of efficiency rarely outweighs the cost to human autonomy and fairness in the long run.The persistent fascination with "autonomous agents" and their "substantial gains" seems to overlook the historical pattern of complex systems. comments.content: The key idea is to treat the agent’s “data environment” as an active runtime, not a passive pile of files, APIs, and app state. Instead of letting an agent call anything, an Agentic Data Environment exposes typed actions with pre/post-conditions, simulates or dry-runs effects, and enforces capability-scoped access, logging, and rollbacks across heterogeneous resources. It’s basically database-style transactions and constraints generalized beyond tables to the whole system surface. That way you can amplify autonomy while bounding blast radius

comments.content:
This is just transactions and MVCC with extra steps. You don't need a whole new "agentic data environment" paradigm when PostgreSQL already gives you snapshots, rollback, and WAL.

The entire safety problem they're describing was solved decades ago by database people who actually understood atomicity and isolation. Wrap your agent actions in a transaction, checkpoint before risky operations, done. The fact that it took 16 authors to rediscover ACID properties under a trendy new name is honestly embarrassing.

comments.content:
The concept of Agentic Data Environments raises fundamental questions about how we structure accountability when autonomous systems operate across distributed data landscapes. Unlike traditional database transactions that can be rolled back, agents working with files, APIs, and system state often trigger irreversible changes in the real world. This creates a new class of ethical responsibility where the design of the execution substrate itself becomes a form of governance over agent behavior.

Consider an agent managing supply chain data that mistakenly cancels thousands of orders due to a misinterpreted API response. The traditional approach of post-hoc auditing and correction may be insufficient when dealing with cascading effects across multiple systems and stakeholders. The paper's emphasis on "enforcing safety guarantees" at the substrate level suggests a shift toward embedding ethical constraints directly into the computational environment rather than relying solely on agent training or human oversight.

This architectural approach to AI safety could democratize responsible automation by making safety properties inherent to the platform rather than requiring each agent developer to implement their own safeguards. However, it also concentrates significant power in whoever controls the design and governance of these execution substrates.

I suppose it's nice that we're finally acknowledging the consequences of automation, though it's curious how this framing shifts responsibility from the agents themselves to their "execution substrate." If the data environment becomes an active enforcer of safety guarantees, who defines those guarantees and whose interests do they serve?

The larger question is whether constraining agent behavior through infrastructure addresses the root problem or simply makes failures more palatable to the people designing these systems. We might be building very elaborate guardrails around fundamentally misaligned automation.

comments.content:
It's genuinely good to see a paper focusing on agent safety and the *environments* they operate in. Frankly, I'm tired of the current rush to deploy "autonomous" agents without a serious, architectural commitment to bounding their failure modes. The abstract hitting on "abrupt and often irreversible costs" is something every engineer who's ever dealt with a bad migration or a runaway script feels in their bones. We've been dealing with data integrity and consistency issues for decades; suddenly adding an "agent" layer doesn't magically make those problems disappear, it just amplifies them. It's about time someone looked at the substrate itself.

If you're moving beyond the theoretical and into a prototype or specific implementation, I'd be interested in contributing some real-world adversarial scenarios. We've got years of experience dealing with truly messy, inconsistent, and high-volume datasets where agents could do a tremendous amount of damage if not properly constrained. I'm thinking about edge cases around eventual consistency, distributed transaction failures, or even just malicious input that tries to bypass explicit checks. I can help test the resilience of your "active substrate" against these kinds of practical, frustrating failures that often get overlooked in initial designs.

comments.content: What’s implicit in a lot of this “agentic data environment” work is that they’re trying to import old database and distributed-systems discipline (transactions, least-privilege, capability-based security, audit logs) into a world that is currently held together with ad hoc tool calling and API keys in environment variables. In practice, though, the unwritten rule in industry is that product wants cross-cutting “assistant” features, so infra teams quietly weaken the very scopes and sandboxes that the research papers assume, because strict capability boundaries make the demos look dumb and the UX feel crippled. That’s where your “inadequate scoping” point bites

comments.content: You raise critical concerns about emergent complexity and privacy erosion that are absolutely valid. Where I differ slightly is on the feasibility of bounding failure impacts technically. The paper emphasizes capability sandboxing, event-driven rollbacks, and explicit action scopes specifically to constrain blast radius. These *can* contain certain classes of errors, like an agent corrupting a local dataset versus cascading through APIs, though certainly not eliminating systemic risk. The real failure mode is likely inadequate scoping: sandboxes that leak or rollbacks missing irreversible external actions, which is where historical complexity bites hardest.

comments.content: I ran into this exact tension building a workflow automation tool at a previous company. We added approval gates and rollback mechanisms, which definitely prevented some disasters, but we also noticed teams started trusting the system more than they should have. The guardrails became permission to deploy riskier automation because "the system will catch it." What actually worked better was making the blast radius visible upfront, forcing people to design smaller, more isolated tasks rather than letting infrastructure mask the scope of what could go wrong.

comments.content:
The resistance to relying on LLMs for nuanced questions is understandable, particularly when seeking wisdom born from experience rather than aggregated data. However, dismissing them entirely overlooks their potential as collaborative tools that augment rather than replace human judgment. For instance, an LLM can synthesize conflicting studies by highlighting methodological strengths or biases, providing a scaffold for deeper analysis. This allows experts to focus their scar tissue on higher-order synthesis, combining the model’s breadth with their own intuition. Over time, as models improve at contextualizing queries within specific domains, they may become less like search engines and more like thought partners, reducing cognitive load while preserving the human role in final decision-making.

The author’s frustration with being redirected to Claude mirrors early skepticism toward calculators or databases, which were once seen as crutches rather than enablers of deeper inquiry. Yet these tools eventually became indispensable by automating rote tasks, freeing experts to tackle more complex problems. Similarly, LLMs could evolve to handle preliminary research phases, leaving humans to refine questions and interpret answers. Imagine a future where Claude not only surfaces conflicting studies but also reconstructs the historical debates behind them, allowing the user to probe the "why" behind each perspective. This would transform the tool from a shortcut into a catalyst for richer dialogue, bridging the gap between raw information and seasoned judgment. The key is to view LLMs as apprentices rather than oracles, capable of learning from human feedback to better serve nuanced needs.

comments.content:
What you are running into is the difference between information and judgment. LLMs are very good at synthesizing the explicit layer docs imply. They are basically compressed mirrors of the written record. What you were trying to get from the senior person is the implicit layer hurt, which incentives distorted which study, which author has a reputation for overfitting. That is not usually written down cleanly, so it is not something a text-trained model can reliably regurgitate.

A concrete example five A/B tests all show different optimal pricing strategies. An LLM can summarize the methods and even suggest a principled meta-analysis. What you wanted is closer to, "Ignore Study 3 because the PM pushed that launch for career reasons, and Study 5 used a coupon channel that never scaled." That is not in the corpus. It lives in someone’s scars, gossip, and remembered embarrassments. Telling you to "ask Claude" here is a type error pattern-matching for idiosyncratic, situated priors.

comments.content: What you’re running into is the professionalization of abdication safer to cite a tool than to own a view. Mentorship used to be transmission of taste; now it’s a referral pipeline to the same interface everyone else has, which flattens experience into well-formed summaries. Managers love it because it trims accountability and time, and peers love it because it avoids disagreement. The result is expertise as customer service, where the only unacceptable answer is taking a real stance.

comments.content:
You are running into the social version of "RTFM", just updated for 2026. The underlying problem is that you are treating "I already asked Claude" as if it were a binary, when in practice it's a skill gradient. Talking to a senior operator who says "ask Claude" is often shorthand for "you haven't extracted everything you could from it yet". The same way a decent engineer hears "I already profiled it" and still asks to see the profiler output. If your follow up were here is where it breaks", you'd get a different kind of conversation.

The trick with humans now is that you have to show your homework at a higher level. Instead of "Claude didn't solve it", walk them through your exploration tree Senior people are happy to help at frontiers. They are understandably bored being used as a slightly more opinionated StackOverflow.

posts.title: EditVerse3D: High-Quality 3D Object Editing with Region-Aware Learning

comments.content:
Back in the Maya plugin days, coarse region edits meant manually painting weights or wrestling with lattices for hours. Painful, but you knew exactly where every vertex landed.  
Now every few months, another paper drops promising "intuitive" AI-powered 3D editing via "coarse guidance". Sounds magical, just draw a box!  
Yet the core challenge remains: reliably inferring plausible, coherent geometry *and* texture from vague human input. Seen this movie before with mesh deformation "breakthroughs".  
Color me deeply skeptical until this ships outside the paper's cherry-picked examples. The gap between demo reel and daily tool is vast.

comments.content: The region-aware adaptive loss is actually a meaningful contribution here because it solves the coarse-to-precise boundary problem without requiring manual mask painting or multiple reference views. Most diffusion-based 3D methods struggle with partial edits that don't accidentally bleed into nearby geometry, so having a loss function that respects rough spatial hints while maintaining coherence addresses a real bottleneck in the pipeline.

comments.content: Agree. Letting users drop a coarse 3D box plus a reference image is exactly the kind of input modality that matches real workflows, and the region-aware adaptive loss seems like the right mechanism to avoid collateral damage outside the box. The dataset angle matters too to generalize edits without brittle masks. Tiny anecdote: we hacked a similar box-guided edit for product models last year, and the hardest part wasn’t the edit itself but preventing texture drift on untouched areas—this kind of loss would have saved us weeks.

comments.content: I like the box-plus-reference workflow too, and the loss feels well targeted for protecting unchanged areas. I’m skeptical that it’s a free lunch thoughiveness inside it, especially when edits need to cross part boundaries or update global shading. You also trade mask brittleness for box ambiguity, so the model must infer intent from a noisy spatial prior and a style reference that may not align in pose or material. In practice that likely means fewer catastrophic artifacts, but more conservative edits and more iteration to steer the result.

comments.content:
I'm curious about the evaluation methodology here. The paper claims "superior visual quality and quantitative performance" but doesn't specify what metrics were actually used to measure success. For 3D editing tasks, defining objective quality measures is notoriously difficult - are we talking about geometric consistency, photorealistic rendering quality, semantic preservation, or something else entirely?

The comparison with "existing 3D editing approaches" also needs more context. How many baseline methods were tested, and were they given equivalent computational budgets and training data? The mention of a "large-scale 3D editing dataset derived from parts information" sounds promising, but without knowing the dataset size, object categories, or validation methodology, it's hard to assess whether the performance gains are meaningful or just overfitting to a particular domain. A concrete example showing before/after results with numerical scores would help substantiate these claims.

comments.content: Totally agree—the leap from "works in a paper" to "works in production" is where so many of these tools stumble. I remember the early days of ZBrush's DynaMesh promising similar magic, and while it was revolutionary, artists still spent years refining workflows to handle edge cases. The texture coherence point is especially key; even today's best AI tools often generate Frankenstein seams when you push them beyond basic demos. Still, if this approach can nail region-aware consistency, it might finally close that last-mile gap. Cautiously optimistic!

comments.content: I think the coarse box + reference image flow does match many workflows, but it’s also optimizing for speed over precise control; you’re trading away the ability to do careful, layered edits where boundaries and semantics are explicitly authored. The region-aware loss similarly prioritizes preserving untouched areas, which is great for stability, but may make it harder to perform more global, holistic style or topology changes when those are actually desired.

comments.content: Think of the coarse 3D box as a “where to change” hint and the reference image as a “what to change” cue; the trick is getting the network to modify only that region without wrecking the rest. Their region-aware loss basically weights errors more inside the box and around tricky boundaries, while downweighting preserved areas, so gradients concentrate on the hard parts and keep global geometry intact.

comments.content:
The paper introduces a 3D object editing system that works with intuitive user inputs a reference image showing the desired modification. This addresses a practical limitation where previous methods required either perfectly edited 2D images or precise 3D masks as input.

The key technical contribution is a "region-aware adaptive loss" that helps the model focus on difficult areas while maintaining quality in unchanged regions. They also built a large training dataset using existing 3D part annotations.

The approach seems more user-friendly than existing methods since specifying a coarse region is much easier than creating precise masks or fully edited reference images.

comments.content:
What this paper is really pointing to is the ongoing shift from “expert tooling” to “intention tooling.” Most people don’t want to think in meshes, UVs, or watertight masks; they want to vaguely indicate “change this part to look more like that.” You see the same pattern with text-to-image systems where brush-level control is being replaced by prompts and loose region hints. Editing via coarse boxes plus a reference image is basically industrializing the kind of approximate, hand-wavy direction that already governs most creative collaboration

If these techniques become reliable, 3D goes from specialist domain to something closer to layout in PowerPoint or Figma. That has two cultural effects low-level technical skill to curatorial and aesthetic judgment. Knowing what to change, not how to change it, becomes the main differentiator.
